per_question.notes: All four target anchors present (¶10, ¶12-20 Read biography intact, ¶23-31 Fuscone arc verbatim, ¶38 'soft skill where how you behave is more important than what you know'). Figure 1 reinforces the contrast visually before the prose. Full thesis recoverable.

The full Markowitz-portfolio fact lives in the figcaption rather than the verbatim quote, which is an acceptable solution.

per_question.notes: All seven target anchors confirmed; Postscript is the longest chapter (9.9% of output, mirroring 9.2% of source). Full arc from 1945-1973 broad-based growth through 1980s+ inequality to Trump-era political consequence is recoverable.

verdict.visual_pleasure: The chrome remains genuinely well-considered (Iowan Old Style serif, 66ch measure, 1.65 leading, warm-grey palette with #b54300 accent, drop-cap on chapter openers, auto dark mode, 3px top progress bar, sticky scroll-spy TOC). What iter 2 changes is the page rhythm: 22 figures break up the 836-anchor vertical stack so the eye gets visual punctuation roughly every 30-40 anchors. Mermaid flowcharts, mindmaps, quadrants render with a quiet two-color classDef palette that matches the body accent rather than fighting it; the SVG curves and bars (compounding, long-tail, margin-of-safety) are restrained and on-brand. The body is no longer barren. The remaining miss: still zero pull-quotes (`aside.pullquote`), zero sidenotes (`.sidenote`), zero in-text cross-references (`.xref`) — the CSS defines them and they're never instantiated. So page rhythm is now varied but not yet *layered*. Net: 4 (above the bar, missing the Tufte-style marginalia that would push it to 5).
verdict.navigation: Unchanged from iter 1 in approach but better-justified now that the article is longer (165 min / 36k words, up from 69 min / 15k). Sticky left-rail TOC with all 22 entries, IntersectionObserver scroll-spy, accent-underline current marker, 3px top progress bar all working. Click-to-jump from any TOC entry lands on the chapter heading correctly; the figure now sits between heading and first anchor so you arrive on the diagram, which is a deliberate orienting cue. The longer article makes the sticky TOC genuinely useful (it would have been overkill for 15k words; for 36k it's earning its space). Still missing the in-text `.xref` cross-references, so 'as we saw in chapter 4' still requires a TOC trip. Score 4.
verdict.comprehension: All 10 questions answerable from the artifact alone, no partials. The verbatim-anchor stitching strategy continues to be the right call for a book whose argument lives in memorable phrasings, and the wider net at threshold ≥5 picks up the narrative-setup paragraphs (Heinz Berggruen's biography ch7§¶3-5, Clarence Hughes's actual 1931 dentist visit ch20§¶5, the valet's aspirational fantasy ch9§¶2,4,6) that iter 1 was missing. The one iter-1 partial (Markowitz 50/50) is resolved via the figure caption, which is a clever ancillary channel for a named-entity detail. Compression: 55.6% source coverage with 1.00 comprehension at this question set is, frankly, very strong. Score 5.
verdict.illustration: Resolved. 22 figures rendered (17 Mermaid + 5 inline SVG), one per chapter as the Design Note promised. Mix of types is appropriate to the claims being illustrated: flowcharts for cause/effect (Read vs Fuscone, Ferrari signal bypass, Rich vs Wealthy), mindmaps for input-cluster claims (money-belief inputs, getting vs keeping), quadrants for skill-vs-luck (Gates/Evans/Buffett/Livermore), and SVG for inherently geometric ideas (exponential curves, long-tail distribution, stacked margin-of-safety bars). Each figure has a numbered figcaption that names what the diagram is doing, and several captions carry secondary facts (e.g. Markowitz 50/50 in Fig 12). Visual register is consistent — neutral Mermaid theme, two-color classDef palette echoing the body accent. Two minor knocks: a handful of Mermaid labels show `<br/>` literal-encoded in source (will still render correctly because Mermaid parses HTML entities, but slightly less robust); and the quadrantChart with bracket coordinates (`[0.85, 0.78]`) is non-standard Mermaid syntax that may render as a syntax warning in older Mermaid builds — worth verifying on the live page. Substantively though: design pack delivered. Score 4 (would be 5 if I could confirm all 22 render without warnings on the CDN Mermaid version).
verdict.books_finished_rate: Honest answer: yes, this iteration moves me to *would skip the original*. The reason is not the figures (those are nice, not decisive) — it's that the threshold-5 anchor pass now preserves the narrative *setup* paragraphs around the named anecdotes, so the stories actually function as stories. In iter 1, Heinz Berggruen's name appeared with no biography and Clarence Hughes appeared only as a callback to a story that was never told; both are now properly introduced. Ronald Read's compounding arc reads continuously from ¶12 (Wikipedia entry) through ¶31 (patient vs greedy) without the previous gappy jumps. The chapter-9 Ferrari/valet sequence went from a 5-anchor fragment to an 8-anchor mini-essay with the aspirational-fantasy setup ('You're smart. You're rich. You have taste. You're important. Look at me.') restored. At 36k words / 165 min, this is no longer a Kindle-highlights export — it's a condensed-but-coherent reader. For my purposes (extending working knowledge across many books faster than reading originals), I would now read this *instead of* Housel's original and feel I'd captured >80% of what the book has to offer. The only caveat: 36k words is roughly 70% of the source length, so the compression ratio is no longer aggressive — Housel's book is short to begin with, and at this density a reader who actually enjoys Housel's connective prose might still prefer the original. But for the 'expand working knowledge' use case the build envelope targets, this delivers. Score 4 (genuine pass, not a courtesy bump — I'd recommend this to a friend instead of the original).

new_or_remaining_issues: Still zero pull-quotes, sidenotes, and in-text cross-references — CSS rules defined but never instantiated. Promoting visual_pleasure to 5 would require the Tufte-style marginalia layer.
Mermaid quadrantChart uses bracket-coordinate syntax (`Bill Gates: [0.85, 0.78]`) that is non-standard; verify it renders on the current CDN Mermaid build without warnings.

Compression ratio is now 71% (36k/51k words) — at this density, readers who specifically value Housel's connective prose may still prefer the original. The artifact has shifted from 'aggressive condensation' to 'lightly-pruned highlights with diagrams'; that's the right call for a 51k-word book but worth flagging as a design choice, not a flaw.

Figure-caption-as-fact-carrier (e.g. Markowitz 50/50 in Fig 12) works for comprehension but blurs the artifact's verbatim-only contract. Acceptable, but worth deciding whether figcaptions count as 'editor commentary' for source-fidelity purposes.
